paths.source_text: newton laws of motion is three physical laws [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
newton laws of motion requires forces [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
newton laws of motion is basis for newtonian mechanics [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
isaac newton is author of principia mathematica [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
principia mathematica is published in 1687 [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
classical mechanics is built on newton laws [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
kinematics is mathematical description of motion [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
velocity is derivative of position [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
velocity equation is v equals ds over dt [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
acceleration is derivative of velocity [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
acceleration equation is a equals dv over dt [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
acceleration is second derivative of position [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
position is a vector [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
velocity is a vector [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
acceleration is a vector [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
force is a vector [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
force is not same as power [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
force is not same as pressure [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
mass is not same as weight [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
first law is principle of inertia [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
first law requires no net force [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
inertia is natural behavior to move in straight line [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
inertia is natural behavior at constant speed [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
first law is no privileged inertial observer [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
first law requires no absolute standard of rest [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
momentum is product of mass and velocity [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
momentum equation is p equals mv [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
second law is force equals rate of change of momentum [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
second law equation is F equals dp over dt [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
force equation is F equals ma [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
second law equation is F equals m times dv over dt [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
net force is zero means mechanical equilibrium [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
free body diagram is visual representation of forces [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
gravitational force equation is F equals GMm over r squared [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
gravitational acceleration equation is g equals GM over r squared [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
gravitational acceleration is approximately 9.8 m per s squared [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
free fall is acceleration at constant rate [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
free fall acceleration is same for all bodies [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
projectile motion follows parabola shaped trajectory [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
centripetal acceleration equation is a equals v squared over r [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
centripetal force is directed toward center of circle [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
centripetal force equation is mv squared over r [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
moon orbit is approximated by uniform circular motion [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
centripetal force is gravity for orbits [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
third law is action and reaction [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
third law requires equal magnitude opposite direction [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
third law requires forces on different bodies [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
third law is related to conservation of momentum [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
momentum is conserved [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
total momentum is p1 plus p2 [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
simple harmonic motion equation is F equals negative kx [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
harmonic oscillator frequency is square root of k over m [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
pendulum is example of harmonic oscillator [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
pendulum frequency equation is omega equals square root of g over L [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
resonance is driven harmonic oscillator phenomenon [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
energy is classified into kinetic and potential [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
kinetic energy is due to motion [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
potential energy is due to position [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
thermal energy is kinetic energy of atoms and molecules [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
work energy theorem is work equals change in kinetic energy [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
force equation is F equals negative gradient U [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
energy is conserved [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
moment of inertia is analogue of mass for rotation [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
angular momentum is analogue of momentum for rotation [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
torque is analogue of force for rotation [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
angular momentum equation is L equals r cross p [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
torque equation is tau equals r cross F [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
angular momentum is conserved when torque is zero [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
universal gravitation is force proportional to product of masses [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
universal gravitation is force inversely proportional to distance squared [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
orbits are conic sections [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
orbits includes ellipses [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
orbits includes parabolas [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
orbits includes hyperbolas [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
kepler problem is finding orbit shape from inverse square force [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
three body problem has no exact closed form solution [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
lagrangian is difference of kinetic and potential energy [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
lagrangian equation is L equals T minus V [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
kinetic energy equation is T equals half mv squared [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
euler lagrange equation is foundation of lagrangian mechanics [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
hamiltonian is often equal to total energy [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
hamiltonian equation is H equals p squared over 2m plus V [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
coulomb law is similar form to gravitational law [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
coulomb force is proportional to product of charges [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
coulomb force is inversely proportional to distance squared [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
lorentz force equation is F equals qE plus qv cross B [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
special relativity is revision of space and time [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
relativistic momentum equation is p equals m gamma v [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
speed of light is maximum speed [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
newtonian mechanics is approximation for slow speeds [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
general relativity is gravity as curvature of spacetime [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
einstein field equations requires tensor calculus [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
sensitive dependence on initial conditions is chaos [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
double pendulum is example of chaos [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
navier stokes equation is newton second law for fluids [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
aristotle is predecessor of newton [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
galileo is credited with concept of inertia [source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion]
